title: After 20 Years, Gaza Votes — But Just One City, and It's Complicated
summary:
Imagine your town hasn't had an election in over two decades. That's the reality for Palestinians in Gaza, but on April 25, 2026, a small slice of change arrived. In Deir al-Balah, a central Gaza city damaged by airstrikes but spared a ground invasion, more than 70,000 eligible voters lined up at polling stations set up in tents and repurposed buildings. It was the first election in part of Gaza since 2006 — a symbolic "pilot," as election officials called it, aimed at politically linking Gaza with the occupied West Bank.

Why does this matter? After more than two years of war, Gaza is devastated. Public services like water, electricity, and roads are nearly nonexistent. Voters said they were compelled to participate because they want a say in rebuilding their lives. "I came to vote because I have a right to elect members to municipal council so they can provide us with services," said Ashraf Abu Dan outside his polling place.

But the election wasn't smooth. The Central Election Commission, based in Ramallah in the West Bank, said Israel blocked the entry of materials like ballot paper, ballot boxes, and ink. So they improvised: using wooden ballot boxes and blue ink left over from a vaccination drive. The commission did not coordinate with Israel or Hamas ahead of the vote. COGAT, the Israeli military body overseeing humanitarian affairs in Gaza, did not respond to questions about blocking materials.

The election also reflects deeper political tensions. The Palestinian Authority, led by 90-year-old President Mahmoud Abbas, promoted the local races after reforms demanded by international donors. But critics say the process sidelines Hamas and other factions. In many West Bank cities, slates are dominated by Fatah, the party that leads the Palestinian Authority. For the first time in six local elections, no other faction put forward its own slate — a sign of political disillusionment.

Turnout may indicate public trust. Historically, Palestinian local elections have drawn 50-60% of voters — high by regional standards (Lebanon's recent local elections saw under 40% turnout; Tunisia's was 12%). But in Qalqilya, a city where no slates registered, voter Marwan Ennabi said, "This isn't transparency. This is chaos, chaos, chaos!" The Palestinian Authority will appoint councils in cities without contested races.

Despite the challenges, the election is a step. As Rami Hamdallah, the commission's chairman, said, "We're talking about geographically linking the West Bank and Gaza Strip." For now, it's a small vote in a single city — but for many Palestinians, it's a chance to be heard.
why_it_matters: This story matters because it shows how difficult it is to hold fair elections in a war-torn region, where political divisions and occupation can prevent people from having a real voice in rebuilding their communities. It also raises questions about whether democracy can survive when trust in institutions has been shattered by years of conflict.

keywords.explanation: Having the right to vote or be chosen for something.
Made or done with whatever is available, without proper materials.
A feeling of disappointment after learning the truth about something you believed in.
Relating to a city or town government.
The number of people who vote in an election.
A small test or trial run before a bigger project.

questions.question: How many years had passed since the last election in part of Gaza before this one?
What city held the election in Gaza?
What did the Central Election Commission use instead of official ballot boxes?
Which group did the Palestinian Authority's election process reportedly sideline?
What was the turnout percentage for Palestinian local elections historically?
What did voter Marwan Ennabi call the election in Qalqilya?

background_read: The Palestinian territories are split between the West Bank (a land occupied by Israel since 1967) and the Gaza Strip (controlled by the militant group Hamas since 2007).

No nationwide elections have been held in Palestine since 2006, partly because of the division between the Palestinian Authority (the government led by President Mahmoud Abbas, based in the West Bank) and Hamas.
The Central Election Commission, based in Ramallah in the West Bank, oversees Palestinian elections but often faces obstacles from Israel, which controls entry points into Gaza.
Local elections in Palestinian areas have historically been seen as a way to measure public trust; low turnout or lack of competing candidates can signal political disengagement.
Article_Structure: LEAD: First election in part of Gaza in 20 years, in Deir al-Balah
└─ DETAIL: 70,000+ eligible voters, setup in tents and repurposed buildings
WHY IT MATTERS: Gaza devastated by war – need for services drives people to vote
└─ QUOTE: Ashraf Abu Dan: 'I have a right to elect members...'
ELECTION LOGISTICS: Central Election Commission improvised because Israel blocked materials
└─ METHOD: Wooden boxes, blue ink from vaccination drive
└─ NO COORDINATION: With Israel or Hamas
POLITICAL TENSIONS: PA promoted elections after donor demands, but process sidelines Hamas
└─ DOMINANCE: Fatah slates only; no other factions field candidates
TURNOUT / TRUST: Historically 50-60% turnout, but Qalqilya had no slates – voter calls it 'chaos'
FORWARD-LOOKING: Commission chairman sees it as linking West Bank and Gaza politically
└─ OPEN QUESTION: Will this pilot expand to other cities?

perspectives.description: For the voters who turned out, the election was a rare chance to choose local leaders who might fix basic services like water and electricity. It gave a sense of agency after years of war.

Critics argue the election was not truly representative because it excluded Hamas and other major factions. The lack of competition in many cities and the chaos in Qalqilya suggest the process may be more about political optics than meaningful change.

The election reflects the deep split in Palestinian governance: the Palestinian Authority in the West Bank versus Hamas in Gaza. Holding a vote in just one city is a symbolic first step, but it also highlights how hard it is to reunite the territories under a single political system.

If the pilot succeeds, it could be expanded to other Gaza cities and help reestablish a political link between Gaza and the West Bank. However, it faces major hurdles: Israeli restrictions, internal Palestinian divisions, and the immense destruction of war.
